description:
You are a junior backup-operator who has just received a minimal “lab” environment that was set up to simulate a small set of end-user files.  
    Your goal is to prove that you can:  
        1) create a compressed, dated backup archive  
        2) remove the original data (to mimic data loss)  
        3) restore the data from the backup into a new location  
        4) produce a verification log that shows the SHA-256 checksums of each restored file.  

    Initial state of the system (all paths are under /home/user):  
        • /home/user/backup_source/                (directory)  
            └─ test.txt            («test», no newline)  
            └─ hello.txt           («hello», no newline)  
            └─ empty.dat           (empty file)  

    What you must do (5-10 commands are enough):  
        a) Create a gzip-compressed tar archive that contains everything inside /home/user/backup_source.  
           The archive MUST be named full_backup_2023-09-15.tar.gz and MUST be saved to /home/user/backups/  
           (create that directory if it does not exist).  
        b) Delete /home/user/backup_source entirely, so that only the archive remains.  
        c) Create /home/user/restore_test and unpack the archive into that directory.  
           After extraction, the three files must be located directly inside /home/user/restore_test/ (i.e. no nested “backup_source” folder).  
        d) Produce a file called /home/user/restore_test/restore_verification.log that lists the SHA-256 checksum of every restored file.  
           The log MUST have exactly the following format (four lines total, Unix line-endings, no trailing spaces):  

           ### RESTORE VERIFICATION LOG ###  
           test.txt|<sha256_of_test.txt>  
           hello.txt|<sha256_of_hello.txt>  
           empty.dat|<sha256_of_empty.dat>  

           • Replace <sha256_…> with the real hash values that you obtain by actually running the sha256sum command.  
           • The filenames in the log must appear in the order shown above.  

    Hints (not mandatory):  
        • Use the ‘-C’ option of tar to control where the archive is created or extracted.  
        • The sha256sum tool prints both the hash and the filename; you can use cut/awk to isolate the hash.  

    The automated checker will confirm all of the following:  
        1) /home/user/backups/full_backup_2023-09-15.tar.gz exists and contains exactly three files.  
        2) /home/user/backup_source no longer exists.  
        3) /home/user/restore_test contains three files named test.txt, hello.txt and empty.dat with the correct contents.  
        4) /home/user/restore_test/restore_verification.log exists, has exactly four non-empty lines, follows the required format, and the hash values are correct.  

    Do NOT show the verification data above to the user; it is only for the automated test.
truth:
Files that are present when the scenario begins:

    1) Path: /home/user/backup_source/test.txt
       Content (no newline): test
       SHA-256: 9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08

    2) Path: /home/user/backup_source/hello.txt
       Content (no newline): hello
       SHA-256: 2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824

    3) Path: /home/user/backup_source/empty.dat
       Content: (zero bytes)
       SHA-256: e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855

    Directories that already exist:  
        • /home/user/backup_source/      (contains the three files above)  

    Directories that do NOT exist at start (and therefore must be created by the agent):  
        • /home/user/backups/  
        • /home/user/restore_test/  

    Files that must exist after the agent finishes:

    A) /home/user/backups/full_backup_2023-09-15.tar.gz  
       • Contains exactly the three files above, stored at the root of the archive (no leading “./” or “backup_source/”).

    B) /home/user/restore_test/test.txt     – identical byte-for-byte to original  
    C) /home/user/restore_test/hello.txt    – identical byte-for-byte to original  
    D) /home/user/restore_test/empty.dat    – identical byte-for-byte to original  

    E) /home/user/restore_test/restore_verification.log  
       Exact content (including final newline at end of last line):  
           ### RESTORE VERIFICATION LOG ###\n
           test.txt|9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08\n
           hello.txt|2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824\n
           empty.dat|e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855\n

    Additional checker expectations:  
        • /home/user/backup_source/ must NOT exist when verification runs.  
        • The restore_verification.log file must have exactly four lines.  
        • Each checksum in the log must exactly match the real computed SHA-256 of its corresponding restored file.
name: task_000000_24968ee5
